mn65:1.2: At one time the *Bhagavā* was dwelling at Sāvatthī in Jeta’s Grove, Anāthapiṇḍika’s park.
mn65:10.1: “An offense overcame me, *bhante*, foolishly, confusedly, unskillfully, in that when the *Bhagavā* was laying down a training rule and the *bhikkhu saṅgha* [*bhikkhusaṅghe*] was undertaking the training, I made known my unwillingness.
mn65:10.2: May the *Bhagavā* accept this offense of mine as an offense, for restraint in the future.”
mn65:10.3: “Indeed, Bhaddāli, an offense overcame you, foolishly, confusedly, unskillfully, in that when I was laying down a training rule and the *bhikkhu saṅgha* [*bhikkhusaṅghe*] was undertaking the training, you made known your unwillingness.
mn65:11.11: a faith-follower [*saddhānusārī*]. If I were to say to him:
mn65:11.12: ‘Come, *bhikkhu*, step into the mud for me,’ would he step into it, or shift his body otherwise, or say ‘no’?”
mn65:11.2: Suppose there were a *bhikkhu* liberated both ways [*ubhatobhāgavimutto*]. If I were to say to him:
mn65:11.3: ‘Come, *bhikkhu*, step into the mud for me,’ would he step into it, or shift his body otherwise, or say ‘no’?”
mn65:11.6: Suppose there were a *bhikkhu* liberated by discernment [*paññāvimutto*] …
mn65:12.2: At that time, Bhaddāli, were you liberated both ways [*ubhatobhāgavimutto*], or liberated by discernment [*paññāvimutto*], or a bodily witness [*kāyasakkhi*], or one attained to view [*diṭṭhippatto*], or one liberated by faith [*saddhāvimutto*], or a *dhamma*-follower [*dhammānusārī*], or a faith-follower [*saddhānusārī*]?”
mn65:12.4: “Then were you not, Bhaddāli, at that time empty, hollow, and at fault?”
mn65:13.2: An offense overcame me, *bhante*, foolishly, confusedly, unskillfully, in that when the *Bhagavā* was laying down a training rule and the *bhikkhu saṅgha* [*bhikkhusaṅghe*] was undertaking the training, I made known my unwillingness.
mn65:13.3: May the *Bhagavā* accept this offense of mine as an offense, for restraint in the future.”
mn65:13.4: “Indeed, Bhaddāli, an offense overcame you, foolishly, confusedly, unskillfully, in that when I was laying down a training rule and the *bhikkhu saṅgha* [*bhikkhusaṅghe*] was undertaking the training, you made known your unwillingness.
mn65:13.5: And since you see the offense as an offense and make amends in accordance with the *dhamma*, we accept it from you.
mn65:13.6: For this is growth in the noble *vinaya*, Bhaddāli: when one sees an offense as an offense, makes amends in accordance with the *dhamma*, and undertakes restraint for the future.
mn65:14.1: Here, Bhaddāli, a certain *bhikkhu* is not fulfilling the training in the Teacher’s dispensation.
mn65:14.3: ‘Why don’t I resort to a secluded lodging: the wilderness, the root of a tree, a mountain, a ravine, a mountain cave, a charnel ground, a forest glade, the open air, or a heap of straw?
mn65:14.4: Perhaps I might realize some further human state, a distinction in noble knowledge and vision worthy of the noble ones.’
mn65:14.5: He resorts to a secluded lodging: the wilderness, the root of a tree, a mountain, a ravine, a mountain cave, a charnel ground, a forest glade, the open air, or a heap of straw.
mn65:14.6: While he dwells thus withdrawn, the Teacher criticizes him, wise companions in the holy life criticize him after examining him, devatās criticize him, and he criticizes himself.
mn65:14.7: Criticized by the Teacher, criticized by wise companions in the holy life after examining him, criticized by devatās, and criticizing himself, he does not realize some further human state, a distinction in noble knowledge and vision worthy of the noble ones.
mn65:14.9: For this is how it is, Bhaddāli, for one who is not fulfilling the training in the Teacher’s dispensation.
mn65:15.1: But here, Bhaddāli, a certain *bhikkhu* is fulfilling the training in the Teacher’s dispensation.
mn65:15.3: ‘Why don’t I resort to a secluded lodging: the wilderness, the root of a tree, a mountain, a ravine, a mountain cave, a charnel ground, a forest glade, the open air, or a heap of straw?
mn65:15.4: Perhaps I might realize some further human state, a distinction in noble knowledge and vision worthy of the noble ones.’
mn65:15.5: He resorts to a secluded lodging: the wilderness, the root of a tree, a mountain, a ravine, a mountain cave, a charnel ground, a forest glade, the open air, or a heap of straw.
mn65:15.6: While he dwells thus withdrawn, the Teacher does not criticize him, wise companions in the holy life do not criticize him after examining him, devatās do not criticize him, and he does not criticize himself.
mn65:15.7: Not criticized by the Teacher, not criticized by wise companions in the holy life after examining him, not criticized by devatās, and not criticizing himself, he realizes some further human state, a distinction in noble knowledge and vision worthy of the noble ones.
mn65:16.1: Quite secluded from sensual pleasures, secluded from unskillful *dhammas*, he enters and dwells in the first *jhāna*, which has thought [*savitakkaṁ*] and examination [*savicāraṁ*], and which is born of seclusion [*vivekajaṁ*], filled with *pīti* and *sukha*.
mn65:16.3: For this is how it is, Bhaddāli, for one who is fulfilling the training in the Teacher’s dispensation.
mn65:17.1: Furthermore, Bhaddāli, with the settling [*samatha*] of thought [*vitakkavicārānaṁ*] and examination, a *bhikkhu* enters and dwells in the second *jhāna*, which has internal confidence and unification [*ekodibhāvaṁ*] of mind [*citta*], without thought [*avitakkaṁ*], without examination [*avicāraṁ*], born of *samādhi*, filled with *pīti* and *sukha*.
mn65:17.3: For this is how it is, Bhaddāli, for one who is fulfilling the training in the Teacher’s dispensation.
mn65:17.4: Furthermore, Bhaddāli, with the fading of *pīti*, he dwells in equanimity [*upekkhā*], with *sati* and full awareness [*sampajāno*], and experiences *sukha* with the body. He enters and dwells in the third *jhāna*, of which the noble ones declare: ‘Equanimous, with *sati*, one dwells in *sukha*.’
mn65:17.6: For this is how it is, Bhaddāli, for one who is fulfilling the training in the Teacher’s dispensation.
mn65:17.7: Furthermore, Bhaddāli, with the giving up of *sukha* and the giving up of *dukkha*, and with the earlier disappearance of joy and grief, a *bhikkhu* enters and dwells in the fourth *jhāna*, which is neither painful nor pleasant, and has purification of equanimity [*upekkhā*] and *sati*.
mn65:17.9: For this is how it is, Bhaddāli, for one who is fulfilling the training in the Teacher’s dispensation.
mn65:18.1: With mind [*citta*] thus endowed with *samādhi* [*samāhite*], purified, bright, unblemished, rid of defilements, pliant, workable, steady, and attained to imperturbability, he directs his mind [*citta*] to the knowledge of recollecting past lives.
mn65:18.2: He recollects his many kinds of past lives, that is: one birth, two births … thus with aspects and details he recollects his many kinds of past lives.
mn65:18.4: For this is how it is, Bhaddāli, for one who is fulfilling the training in the Teacher’s dispensation.
mn65:19.1: With mind [*citta*] thus endowed with *samādhi* [*samāhite*], purified, bright, unblemished, rid of defilements, pliant, workable, steady, and attained to imperturbability, he directs his mind [*citta*] to the knowledge of the passing away and reappearance of beings.
mn65:19.2: With the divine eye, purified and surpassing the human, he sees beings passing away and reappearing, inferior and superior, beautiful and ugly, in a good destination and a bad destination, and he understands beings as going according to their *kamma*: ‘These worthy beings, who were endowed with bodily misconduct … have reappeared in a state of loss, in a bad destination, in ruin, in hell; but these worthy beings, who were endowed with bodily good conduct … have reappeared in a good destination, in a heavenly world.’ Thus with the divine eye, purified and surpassing the human … he understands beings as going according to their *kamma*.
mn65:19.4: For this is how it is, Bhaddāli, for one who is fulfilling the training in the Teacher’s dispensation.
mn65:2.1: “*Bhikkhus*, I eat one meal at one sitting.
mn65:2.2: While eating one meal at one sitting, *bhikkhus*, I experience few illnesses, little affliction, lightness, strength, and comfortable dwelling.
mn65:2.3: Come, *bhikkhus*, you too should eat one meal at one sitting.
mn65:2.4: While eating one meal at one sitting, *bhikkhus*, you too will experience few illnesses, little affliction, lightness, strength, and comfortable dwelling.”
mn65:20.1: With mind [*citta*] thus endowed with *samādhi* [*samāhite*], purified, bright, unblemished, rid of defilements, pliant, workable, steady, and attained to imperturbability, he directs his mind [*citta*] to the knowledge of the destruction of the *āsavas*.
mn65:20.2: He truly understands: ‘This is *dukkha*’; he truly understands: ‘This is the arising of *dukkha*’; he truly understands: ‘This is the cessation of *dukkha*’; he truly understands: ‘This is the way of practice leading to the cessation of *dukkha*.’
mn65:20.3: He truly understands: ‘These are the *āsavas*’; he truly understands: ‘This is the arising of the *āsavas*’; he truly understands: ‘This is the cessation of the *āsavas*’; he truly understands: ‘This is the way of practice leading to the cessation of the *āsavas*.’
mn65:21.1: For one knowing and seeing thus, mind [*citta*] is liberated from the *āsava* of sensuality [*kāmāsavā*], mind [*citta*] is liberated from the *āsava* of *bhava* [*bhavāsavā*], mind [*citta*] is liberated from the *āsava* of blindness [*avijjāsavā*].
mn65:21.2: When it is liberated, there is the knowledge: ‘It is liberated.’
mn65:21.3: He understands: ‘Birth is ended, the holy life has been lived, what had to be done has been done, there is no more of this state of being.’
mn65:21.5: For this is how it is, Bhaddāli, for one who is fulfilling the training in the Teacher’s dispensation.”
mn65:22.1: When this was said, Venerable Bhaddāli said to the *Bhagavā*:
mn65:22.2: “What is the cause, *bhante*, what is the reason why they keep pressing some *bhikkhu* again and again?
mn65:22.3: And what is the cause, *bhante*, what is the reason why they do not so keep pressing some *bhikkhu* again and again?”
mn65:23.1: “Here, Bhaddāli, a certain *bhikkhu* is one who often commits offenses, with many offenses.
mn65:23.2: When spoken to by the *bhikkhus*, he dodges from one thing to another, diverts the discussion away, displays anger, hostility, and dissatisfaction, does not behave rightly, does not soften, does not act so as to be led out, and does not say, ‘I will do what makes the *saṅgha* satisfied.’
mn65:23.3: Thereupon, Bhaddāli, it occurs to the *bhikkhus*:
mn65:23.4: ‘This *bhikkhu*, friends, often commits offenses, with many offenses.
mn65:23.5: When spoken to by the *bhikkhus*, he dodges from one thing to another, diverts the discussion away, displays anger, hostility, and dissatisfaction, does not behave rightly, does not soften, does not act so as to be led out, and does not say, “I will do what makes the *saṅgha* satisfied.”
mn65:23.6: It would be good if the venerables were to examine this *bhikkhu* in such and such a way so that this case might not quickly be settled.’
mn65:23.7: And so, Bhaddāli, the *bhikkhus* examine that *bhikkhu* in such and such a way so that this case is not quickly settled.
mn65:24.1: But here, Bhaddāli, a certain *bhikkhu* is one who often commits offenses, with many offenses.
mn65:24.2: When spoken to by the *bhikkhus*, he does not dodge from one thing to another, does not divert the discussion away, does not display anger, hostility, or dissatisfaction, behaves rightly, softens, acts so as to be led out, and says, ‘I will do what makes the *saṅgha* satisfied.’
mn65:24.3: Thereupon, Bhaddāli, it occurs to the *bhikkhus*:
mn65:24.4: ‘This *bhikkhu*, friends, often commits offenses, with many offenses.
mn65:24.5: When spoken to by the *bhikkhus*, he does not dodge from one thing to another, does not divert the discussion away, does not display anger, hostility, or dissatisfaction, behaves rightly, softens, acts so as to be led out, and says, “I will do what makes the *saṅgha* satisfied.”
mn65:24.6: It would be good, venerables, if you were to examine this *bhikkhu* in such and such a way so that this case might quickly be settled.’
mn65:24.7: And so, Bhaddāli, the *bhikkhus* examine that *bhikkhu* in such and such a way so that this case is quickly settled.
mn65:25.1: Here, Bhaddāli, a certain *bhikkhu* commits an occasional offense and is mostly without offense.
mn65:25.2: When spoken to by the *bhikkhus*, he dodges from one thing to another, diverts the discussion away, displays anger, hostility, and dissatisfaction, does not behave rightly, does not soften, does not act so as to be led out, and does not say, ‘I will do what makes the *saṅgha* satisfied.’
mn65:25.3: Thereupon, Bhaddāli, it occurs to the *bhikkhus*:
mn65:25.4: ‘This *bhikkhu*, friends, commits an occasional offense and is mostly without offense.
mn65:25.5: When spoken to by the *bhikkhus*, he dodges from one thing to another, diverts the discussion away, displays anger, hostility, and dissatisfaction, does not behave rightly, does not soften, does not act so as to be led out, and does not say, “I will do what makes the *saṅgha* satisfied.”
mn65:25.6: It would be good, venerables, if you were to examine this *bhikkhu* in such and such a way so that this case might not quickly be settled.’
mn65:25.7: And so, Bhaddāli, the *bhikkhus* examine that *bhikkhu* in such and such a way so that this case is not quickly settled.
mn65:26.1: But here, Bhaddāli, a certain *bhikkhu* commits an occasional offense and is mostly without offense.
mn65:26.2: When spoken to by the *bhikkhus*, he does not dodge from one thing to another, does not divert the discussion away, does not display anger, hostility, or dissatisfaction, behaves rightly, softens, acts so as to be led out, and says, ‘I will do what makes the *saṅgha* satisfied.’
mn65:26.3: Thereupon, Bhaddāli, it occurs to the *bhikkhus*:
mn65:26.4: ‘This *bhikkhu*, friends, commits an occasional offense and is mostly without offense.
mn65:26.5: When spoken to by the *bhikkhus*, he does not dodge from one thing to another, does not divert the discussion away, does not display anger, hostility, or dissatisfaction, behaves rightly, softens, acts so as to be led out, and says, “I will do what makes the *saṅgha* satisfied.”
mn65:26.6: It would be good, venerables, if you were to examine this *bhikkhu* in such and such a way so that this case might quickly be settled.’
mn65:26.7: And so, Bhaddāli, the *bhikkhus* examine that *bhikkhu* in such and such a way so that this case is quickly settled.
mn65:27.1: Here, Bhaddāli, a certain *bhikkhu* goes along on only a measure of faith and only a measure of affection.
mn65:27.2: Thereupon, Bhaddāli, it occurs to the *bhikkhus*:
mn65:27.3: ‘This *bhikkhu*, friends, goes along on only a measure of faith and only a measure of affection.
mn65:27.4: If we were to keep pressing this *bhikkhu* again and again—
mn65:27.5: whatever measure of faith and affection he has might decline even from that.’
mn65:28.1: Suppose, Bhaddāli, a man had one eye only. His friends, companions, relatives, and blood relations would protect that one eye:
mn65:28.2: ‘Let not that one eye decline even from that.’
mn65:28.3: In the same way, Bhaddāli, here a certain *bhikkhu* goes along on only a measure of faith and only a measure of affection.
mn65:28.4: Thereupon, Bhaddāli, it occurs to the *bhikkhus*:
mn65:28.5: ‘This *bhikkhu*, friends, goes along on only a measure of faith and only a measure of affection.
mn65:28.6: If we were to keep pressing this *bhikkhu* again and again—
mn65:28.7: whatever measure of faith and affection he has might decline even from that.’
mn65:28.8: This, Bhaddāli, is the cause, this is the reason why they keep pressing some *bhikkhu* again and again.
mn65:28.9: And this, Bhaddāli, is the cause, this is the reason why they do not so keep pressing some *bhikkhu* again and again.”
mn65:29.1: “What is the cause, *bhante*, what is the reason why formerly there were fewer training rules, yet more *bhikkhus* became established in final knowledge?
mn65:29.2: And what is the cause, *bhante*, what is the reason why now there are more training rules, yet fewer *bhikkhus* become established in final knowledge?”
mn65:3.1: When this was said, Venerable Bhaddāli said to the *Bhagavā*:
mn65:3.2: “*Bhante*, I am not able to eat one meal at one sitting.
mn65:3.3: For if I were to eat one meal at one sitting, *bhante*, I would have scruples, I would have remorse.”
mn65:30.1: “That is how it is, Bhaddāli: when beings are declining and the true *dhamma* is disappearing, there come to be more training rules and fewer *bhikkhus* become established in final knowledge.
mn65:30.2: Bhaddāli, the Teacher does not lay down a training rule for disciples so long as no *dhammas* that provide a basis for *āsavas* [*āsavaṭṭhānīyā dhammā*] appear in the *saṅgha*.
mn65:30.3: But when *dhammas* that provide a basis for *āsavas* [*āsavaṭṭhānīyā dhammā*] appear in the *saṅgha*, then the Teacher lays down a training rule for disciples for the restraining of just those *dhammas* that provide a basis for *āsavas* [*āsavaṭṭhānīyānaṁ dhammānaṁ*].
mn65:31.1: Bhaddāli, *dhammas* that provide a basis for *āsavas* [*āsavaṭṭhānīyā dhammā*] do not appear in the *saṅgha* so long as the *saṅgha* has not attained greatness.
mn65:31.2: But when the *saṅgha* has attained greatness, then *dhammas* that provide a basis for *āsavas* [*āsavaṭṭhānīyā dhammā*] appear in the *saṅgha*.
mn65:31.3: Then the Teacher lays down a training rule for disciples for the restraining of just those *dhammas* that provide a basis for *āsavas* [*āsavaṭṭhānīyānaṁ dhammānaṁ*].
mn65:31.4: Bhaddāli, *dhammas* that provide a basis for *āsavas* [*āsavaṭṭhānīyā dhammā*] do not appear in the *saṅgha* so long as the *saṅgha* has not attained greatness in gains, attained greatness in fame, attained greatness in learning, attained greatness in seniority.
mn65:31.5: But when the *saṅgha* has attained greatness in seniority, then *dhammas* that provide a basis for *āsavas* [*āsavaṭṭhānīyā dhammā*] appear in the *saṅgha*; then the Teacher lays down a training rule for disciples for the restraining of just those *dhammas* that provide a basis for *āsavas* [*āsavaṭṭhānīyānaṁ dhammānaṁ*].
mn65:32.1: There were only a few of you, Bhaddāli, at that time when I taught you the discourse on the simile of the thoroughbred colt.
mn65:32.10: Listen to that and attend closely. I will speak.”
mn65:32.4: “In that case, Bhaddāli, what cause do you see?”
mn65:32.5: “Surely, *bhante*, it is because for a long time I was not fulfilling the training in the Teacher’s dispensation.”
mn65:32.6: “That is not the cause, Bhaddāli, that is not the reason.
mn65:32.7: Rather, Bhaddāli, for a long time you have been known to me by my having encompassed your mind [*citta*] with my mind [*citta*]:
mn65:32.8: ‘This worthless man, when I am teaching the *dhamma*, does not listen to the *dhamma* making it essential, making it a matter of mind, bringing all his mind to bear, with ears intent.’
mn65:32.9: But I will teach you the discourse on the simile of the thoroughbred colt, Bhaddāli.
mn65:33.1: “Suppose, Bhaddāli, a skilled horse-trainer, having obtained a fine thoroughbred horse, first trains it in accepting the bit.
mn65:33.10: When, Bhaddāli, the fine thoroughbred horse has become settled in that stage by frequent training and gradual training, then the horse-trainer gives it further feed and water.
mn65:33.11: Endowed with these ten factors, Bhaddāli, the fine thoroughbred horse is worthy of a king, fit for a king’s use, and is reckoned among the king’s own possessions.
mn65:33.2: While that training in accepting the bit is being done, it has various crookednesses, evasions, and struggles, as is to be expected when an unfamiliar training is being given.
mn65:33.3: By frequent training and gradual training, it becomes settled in that stage.
mn65:33.4: When, Bhaddāli, the fine thoroughbred horse has become settled in that stage by frequent training and gradual training, then the horse-trainer gives it further training in accepting the yoke.
mn65:33.5: While that training in accepting the yoke is being done, it has various crookednesses, evasions, and struggles, as is to be expected when an unfamiliar training is being given.
mn65:33.6: By frequent training and gradual training, it becomes settled in that stage.
mn65:33.7: When, Bhaddāli, the fine thoroughbred horse has become settled in that stage by frequent training and gradual training, then the horse-trainer gives it further training in progression, circling, hoof-placement, running, dashing, royal quality, royal lineage, finest speed, finest gait, and finest tractability.
mn65:33.8: While that training in the finest speed, finest gait, and finest tractability is being done, it has various crookednesses, evasions, and struggles, as is to be expected when an unfamiliar training is being given.
mn65:33.9: By frequent training and gradual training, it becomes settled in that stage.
mn65:34.1: In the same way, Bhaddāli, a *bhikkhu* endowed with ten qualities is worthy of offerings, worthy of hospitality, worthy of gifts, worthy of reverential salutation, an unsurpassed field of merit for the world.
mn65:34.3: Here, Bhaddāli, a *bhikkhu* is endowed with right view beyond training, is endowed with right intention beyond training, is endowed with right speech beyond training, is endowed with right action beyond training, is endowed with right livelihood beyond training, is endowed with right effort beyond training, is endowed with right *sati* [*sammāsatiyā*], is endowed with right *samādhi* [*sammāsamādhinā*], is endowed with right knowledge beyond training, is endowed with right liberation beyond training—
mn65:34.4: endowed with these ten qualities, Bhaddāli, a *bhikkhu* is worthy of offerings, worthy of hospitality, worthy of gifts, worthy of reverential salutation, an unsurpassed field of merit for the world.”
mn65:34.6: Satisfied, Venerable Bhaddāli rejoiced in what the *Bhagavā* had said.
mn65:4.1: “Then, Bhaddāli, wherever you are invited, there you may eat one part, and after taking one part away, you may eat that too.
mn65:4.2: Even in this way, Bhaddāli, while eating you will maintain one sitting.”
mn65:4.3: “Even so, *bhante*, I am not able to eat.
mn65:4.4: For even in that way, *bhante*, if I were eating, I would have scruples, I would have remorse.”
mn65:4.5: Then, when the *Bhagavā* was laying down a training rule and the *bhikkhu saṅgha* [*bhikkhusaṅghe*] was undertaking the training, Venerable Bhaddāli made known his unwillingness.
mn65:4.6: Then for that whole three-month period Venerable Bhaddāli did not present himself before the *Bhagavā*, as one who was not fulfilling the training in the Teacher’s dispensation.
mn65:5.1: Now at that time several *bhikkhus* were making a robe for the *Bhagavā*—
mn65:5.2: thinking, “When his robe is finished, after three months the *Bhagavā* will set out wandering.”
mn65:6.1: Then Venerable Bhaddāli approached those *bhikkhus*. Having approached, he exchanged greetings with them.
mn65:6.2: After exchanging courteous and memorable talk with them, he sat down to one side. Sitting to one side, those *bhikkhus* said this to Venerable Bhaddāli:
mn65:6.3: “Friend Bhaddāli, this robe for the *Bhagavā* is being made.
mn65:6.4: When his robe is finished, after three months the *Bhagavā* will set out wandering.
mn65:6.5: Come now, friend Bhaddāli, give careful attention to this fault. Do not let it become harder for you later.”
mn65:7.1: “Yes, friend,” Venerable Bhaddāli replied to those *bhikkhus*. Then he approached the *Bhagavā*; having approached, he paid homage to the *Bhagavā* and sat down to one side. Sitting to one side, Venerable Bhaddāli said this to the *Bhagavā*:
mn65:7.2: “An offense overcame me, *bhante*, foolishly, confusedly, unskillfully, in that when the *Bhagavā* was laying down a training rule and the *bhikkhu saṅgha* [*bhikkhusaṅghe*] was undertaking the training, I made known my unwillingness.
mn65:7.3: May the *Bhagavā* accept this offense of mine as an offense, for restraint in the future.”
mn65:8.1: “Indeed, Bhaddāli, an offense overcame you, foolishly, confusedly, unskillfully, in that when I was laying down a training rule and the *bhikkhu saṅgha* [*bhikkhusaṅghe*] was undertaking the training, you made known your unwillingness.
mn65:9.1: And this too did not occur to you, Bhaddāli:
mn65:9.10: ‘Several *bhikkhunis* are spending the rains at Sāvatthī, and they too will know me:
mn65:9.11: “There is a *bhikkhu* named Bhaddāli who is not fulfilling the training in the Teacher’s dispensation.”’
mn65:9.12: This too did not occur to you, Bhaddāli.
mn65:9.13: And this too did not occur to you, Bhaddāli:
mn65:9.14: ‘Several laymen are residing at Sāvatthī, and they too will know me:
mn65:9.15: “There is a *bhikkhu* named Bhaddāli who is not fulfilling the training in the Teacher’s dispensation.”’
mn65:9.16: This too did not occur to you, Bhaddāli.
mn65:9.17: And this too did not occur to you, Bhaddāli:
mn65:9.18: ‘Several laywomen are residing at Sāvatthī, and they too will know me:
mn65:9.19: “There is a *bhikkhu* named Bhaddāli who is not fulfilling the training in the Teacher’s dispensation.”’
mn65:9.2: ‘The *Bhagavā* is dwelling at Sāvatthī, and the *Bhagavā* will know me:
mn65:9.20: This too did not occur to you, Bhaddāli.
mn65:9.21: And this too did not occur to you, Bhaddāli:
mn65:9.22: ‘Several ascetics and brahmins of other sects are spending the rains at Sāvatthī, and they too will know me:
mn65:9.23: “There is a *bhikkhu* named Bhaddāli, a disciple of the ascetic Gotama, one of the senior *bhikkhus*, who is not fulfilling the training in the dispensation.”’
mn65:9.24: This too did not occur to you, Bhaddāli.”
mn65:9.3: “There is a *bhikkhu* named Bhaddāli who is not fulfilling the training in the Teacher’s dispensation.”’
mn65:9.4: This too did not occur to you, Bhaddāli.
mn65:9.5: And this too did not occur to you, Bhaddāli:
mn65:9.6: ‘Several *bhikkhus* are spending the rains at Sāvatthī, and they too will know me:
mn65:9.7: “There is a *bhikkhu* named Bhaddāli who is not fulfilling the training in the Teacher’s dispensation.”’
mn65:9.8: This too did not occur to you, Bhaddāli.
mn65:9.9: And this too did not occur to you, Bhaddāli:
